coredata.dc:title: Surface functionality and formation mechanisms of carbon and graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Bokare, Anuja
Nordlund, Dennis
Melendrez, Cynthia
Robinson, Ryan
Keles, Ozgur
Wolcott, Abraham
Erogbogbo, Folarin
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Carbon dots (C-dots) have been widely studied in recent years due to their structural and optoelectrical properties. These properties have prompted the exploration of the role of these carbon-based materials in many potential applications. This includes solar cells, photodetectors, bioimaging, sensors, batteries and drug delivery. These properties and applications of GQDs and C-dots are highly dependent on their size, shape and surface functionality. In this work, GQDs and C-dots mixtures have been synthesized by an inexpensive wet chemical method by varying the synthesis temperature from 85°, 100° to 115 °C. The surface functionalities of the synthesized carbon-based materials were investigated by several analytical methods. We discovered a higher degree of oxidation at higher temperatures. The mechanism of formation of different sized GQDs and C-dots with different functionalities have been explained with the help of XPS and NEXAFS analysis. The influence of size and surface functionalities on the optical properties of these nanomaterials is analyzed by UV–Vis and PL spectroscopic techniques. This study demonstrates that physicochemical properties of GQDs and C-dots can be controlled by changing the synthesis temperature.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Near edge X-ray absorption fine structure
X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy
Formation mechanism
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by San Jose State University Central Research, Scholarly and Creative Activity (RSCA) Program and the Charles W. Davidson College of Engineering.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of a) GQD-85 b) GQD-100 c) GQD-115, AFM images and heights from line profile of d) GQD-85 e) GQD-100 f) GQD-115, HRTEM images of g) GQD-85 h) GQD-100 i) GQD-115.
Raman spectra of a) GQD-85 b) GQD-100 c) GQD-115 sample and d) FTIR spectra of all C-dots and GQDs samples.
C-1s XPS spectra of a) GQD-85 b) GQD-100 c) GQD-115 samples and O-1s XPS spectra of d) GQD-85 e) GQD-100 f) GQD-115 sample.
a) C K-edge and b) O K-edge NEXAFS spectra of C-dots and GQDs samples.
Schematic illustration explaining formation of O-functional groups on C-dots and GQDs at different temperatures.
a) UV–Vis b) PL spectra of C-dots and GQDs samples.
% compositions of different functional groups present in the C-dots and GQDs sample determined by C-1s XPS analysis.

row.entry: % compositions of components in C-1s spectra

row.entry: % compositions of components in O-1s spectra

head.ce:title: Surface functionality and formation mechanisms of carbon and graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biomedical Engineering, San Jose State University, 1 Washington Square, San Jose, CA 95112, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biomedical Engineering, San Jose State University, 1 Washington Square, San Jose, California 95112, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Stanford Synchrotron Radiation Light Source, SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory, Menlo Park, CA 94025, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical and Materials Engineering Department, San Jose State University, 1 Washington Square, San Jose, CA 95112, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical and Materials Engineering Department, San Jose State University, 1 Washington Square, San Jose, California 95112, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Carbon dots (C-dots) have been widely studied in recent years due to their structural and optoelectrical properties. These properties have prompted the exploration of the role of these carbon-based materials in many potential applications. This includes solar cells, photodetectors, bioimaging, sensors, batteries and drug delivery. These properties and applications of GQDs and C-dots are highly dependent on their size, shape and surface functionality. In this work, GQDs and C-dots mixtures have been synthesized by an inexpensive wet chemical method by varying the synthesis temperature from 85°, 100° to 115 °C. The surface functionalities of the synthesized carbon-based materials were investigated by several analytical methods. We discovered a higher degree of oxidation at higher temperatures. The mechanism of formation of different sized GQDs and C-dots with different functionalities have been explained with the help of XPS and NEXAFS analysis. The influence of size and surface functionalities on the optical properties of these nanomaterials is analyzed by UV–Vis and PL spectroscopic techniques. This study demonstrates that physicochemical properties of GQDs and C-dots can be controlled by changing the synthesis temperature.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Near edge X-ray absorption fine structure

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of graphene, immense efforts have been focused on the synthesis of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) and C-dots (C-dots) as they have a great combination of characteristics than any other semiconductor quantum dots [1]. These characteristics includes lower toxicity, higher solubility in various solvents, great electronic features, strong chemical inertness, high specific surface areas and plenty of edge sites for functionalization [2–5]. Due to these properties, these Carbon-based materials have great potential for providing new approaches and critical improvements in various fields such as composite fabrication, electrochemistry, drug-delivery, biosensing, cellular imaging, energy storage, and photovoltaics [6–12]. Recently, composites of GQDs with carbon nitride nanorods and two dimensional (2D) hexagonal boron nitride are attracting significant attention in the area of nano-sensors [13,14]. Similarly, GQDs functionalized with the Pt, Pd and Au nanoparticles have been successfully used as a catalyst in electrochemical fuel cell applications [15–17]. However, physicochemical properties of these nanomaterials show discrete changes depending upon their size and surface functionalities [18]. Therefore, controllable synthesis of GQDs and C-dots with desired particle size and functional groups is still a big challenge.

Various approaches have been developed to fabricate GQDs and C-dots with specific sizes and functional groups. These approaches include the use of hydrothermal, solvothermal, sonochemical and microwave assisted methods to cut the graphene sheets to form these Carbon-based nanomaterials [19–22]. These methods use precursor materials such as graphene-oxide (GO). The resulting GQDs frequently have only one emission color [23]. Some other synthesis methods such as electro-beam lithography and ruthenium catalyzed C60 transformation involves extremely expensive raw materials and low yields [24,25]. Among several available methods, two-step acid-oxidation and cutting of graphene sheets has become more popular, due to its simplicity and inexpensiveness [26–28]. GQDs and C-dots with different oxygen levels contents can be obtained through this method by changing the precursor, oxidation time and amount of oxidant [29]. However, no strong link has been established between the properties of these nanomaterials and the temperature of synthesis. This paper reports a systematic study on influence of synthesis temperature on the functional group properties of GQDs and C-dots.

In the present work, mixtures of GQDs and C-dots have been synthesized using very inexpensive and easily available bird-charcoal as a precursor. Bird charcoal has very small domain structure of Sp2 carbon which can be easily oxidized by strong acid treatments. The synthesis has been carried out at different temperatures e.g. 85°, 100° and 115 °C resulting into the formation of different sized GQDs and C-dots which are characterized by TEM analysis. Different functional groups attached to the GQDs and C-dots have been verified by several characterization techniques such as Raman, FTIR, and XPS spectroscopy. Results obtained by these techniques were further confirmed by NEXAFS analysis as the information obtained through NEXAFS corresponds to a depth of few nanometers into the surface and not to the bulk [30]. These results provide a further understanding of effect of temperature on the physicochemical properties of GQDs and C-dots. This will be helpful in the promotion of the fabrication of GQDs with desired size and properties.

Bird charcoal, the starting material for synthesis, was obtained from Eight-in-One Pet Products Co. Ltd. (Islandia, NY). Concentrated sulfuric acid (98% H2SO4), concentrated nitric acid (70% HNO3) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (San Jose, CA). White pellets of Sodium hydroxide were acquired from Fisher Scientific (Pittsburg, PA).

Mixtures of GQDs and C-dots were synthesized by using a bird charcoal as a precursor. Initially, 300 mg of bird charcoal was sonicated for 2 h in a mixture of 60 ml of 98% concentrated sulfuric acid and 20 ml of 70% concentrated nitric acid. This addition of acids enabled breakdown of bonds in the charcoal. The mixture thus formed, was left for heat treatment in hot paraffin oil for 24 h. The temperature of hot oil was varied from 85, 100 °C, to 115 °C. Obtained mixture was cooled to room temperature for 30 min. Once at room temperature, the mixture was poured into a beaker containing 100 ml ice, to which 3 M NaOH was added for bringing pH of the mixture to 7. The addition of NaOH was carried out in ice bath to avoid heat generation during exothermic reaction. The obtained neutral mixture was filtered through a 0.45 mm PTFE syringe filter membrane. The filtrate collected was dialyzed for consecutive 5 days using 1000 Da dialysis bag. The purified sample obtained was concentrated using rotary evaporator. The concentrated solution of GQDs and C-dots dispersed in DI water were stored in refrigerator at a temperature of about 4 °C or below in order to fully preserve the optical properties of GQDs and C-dots. The mixture of GQDs and C-Dots synthesized at 85, 100 and 115 °C are abbreviated as GQD-85, GQD-100, and GQD-115 in further text.

The TEM imaging of the GQDs and C-dots was performed at the NASA AMES facility using Hitachi H-9500 System (operating voltage of the system was 300 kV). GQD and C-dot sample was drop casted on to Lacey Carbon films having a woven-mesh-like pattern. The sample was then allowed to dry for 30 min at room temperature and then viewed under the microscope. AFM topography imaging of GQD and C-dots was performed using model 5500 Keysight Scanning Probe Microscope (SPM) in SJSU (San Jose). A few droplets of the GQD and C-dots solution were placed onto a mica substrate of 1 cm diameter so as to from three to four layered films and allowed to air dry. Composite solutions of different concentration were spin coated onto the mica substrates. The height profile of the mixtures of GQDs and C-dots was obtained from the AFM topography images with a magnification of about 2 μm or more.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared and Raman spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR was performed to study the functionalization of the GQDs and C-dots. A small portion of each sample was transferred to an infrared transmitting substrate and examined using a Thermo-Nicolet 6700 Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrometer equipped with a Continuum microscope in transmission mode. The analytical spot size was approximately 100 μm × 100 μm. OMNIC 8.0 software was used to perform data analysis. Raman spectroscopy was done at BaySpec (Santa Clara, CA) using a 3 in 1 Nomadic Raman Microscope. A green laser with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm was used.

An aqueous dispersion of GQD and C-dots was drop casted on gold-coated Si wafers with a native oxide layer. The wafers were dried at 80 °C under flowing argon. The dried samples were kept in a clean Teflon container before spectroscopic investigations. A beamline at the Stanford Synchrotron Radiation Lightsource (SLAC) National Accelerator Laboratory was used for the NEXAFS and XPS characterization. NEXAFS data were collected at an incident electric field vector of 55°. A linear pre-edge background was subtracted from the spectra before the absorption edge of carbon at 260–280 eV and oxygen at 510–530 eV. We performed a post-edge normalization in the continuum region at 340 eV for carbon and 580 eV for oxygen. XPS was performed with a hemispherical electron spectrometer (SES-R3000, VG-Scienta). The energy calibration was performed using the signal from a gold substrate (Au 4f5/2 = 85.0 eV) after argon ion sputtering under ultrahigh-vacuum conditions (5 × 10–8 Torr). A commercial 5700 PHI electronics X-ray photoelectron spectrometer was used for data collection.

Fig. 1 a, b and c shows the TEM images of GQD-85, GQD-100 and GQD-115 respectively. These images revealed that the density of GQDs and C-dots formed in the solution increased with an increase in the temperature of the synthesis. Fig. 1d, e and f shows the AFM images of GQD-85, GQD-100 and GQD-115 respectively with their heights corresponding to the line profile. The bright spots in the AFM images indicate the presence of nanosized C-dots and GQDs. The line profile measurements for these samples were obtained using Gwyddion software tool. For GQD-85 sample, most of the nanomaterials show height profile above 5 nm. This indicates that this sample contains more percentage of C-dots than the GQDs. A significant fraction of nanoparticles has heights of 2 nm in GQD-100 and GQD-115 samples indicating higher percentage of GQDs than the C-dots. The average heights of GQD-85, GQD-100 and GQD-115 samples were found to be ~8.3 nm, 6.4 and 3.8 nm respectively. This size distribution of these mixtures of GQDs and C-dots clearly shows a pattern of decrease in average height with increase in temperature of synthesis thus confirming the effect of synthesis temperature on controlling the size of GQDs. Fig. 1g, h and i depicts the HRTEM images of the Dots. As can be noticed from these images, the lattice spacing of the GQDs and C-dots is observed to be ~0. 22 nm which emphasize high crystallinity.

Raman spectroscopy is an important tool to deduce the structure related to the nature of defects and disorder in graphene-based materials. Raman spectra for all samples have been recorded and presented in Fig. 2 (a, b and c). As can be observed from Fig. 2(a, b and c), Raman spectra of mixture of GQDs and C-dots samples can be clearly resolved into two distinctive D and G bands around 1580 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 respectively. The graphitic peak (G band) corresponds to the optical E2g phonons at the Brillouin zone center resulting from the bond stretching of sp2 carbon pairs in both, rings and chains. Whereas, disorder (D band) represents the breathing mode of aromatic rings arising due to the defects in the sample [31].

The intensity ratio of the D and G band i.e. (ID/IG ratio) helps to estimate the defects of graphene sample where higher ratio ensures more defects on graphene sample [32]. The calculated ID/IG ratio for GQD-85, GQD-100 and GQD115 samples are 0.96, 1.04 and 1.16 respectively. The higher ratio for GQD-115 sample clearly depicts more defective nature which is may be due to the higher degree of oxidation occurring at higher temperature.

Fig. 2d depicts the FT-IR spectra of GQD-85, GQD-100 and GQD-115 samples. FTIR spectroscopy clearly reveals the presence of different functional groups in these samples. All the spectra show absorption bands at ~3400, 2370, 1720, 1630, 1420, 1115, 1000 and 640 cm−1.

The strongest absorption band exhibited by all GQDs sample between ~1130 cm−1 is associated with stretching vibration of CO bond in hydroxyl groups whereas, a small shoulder peak ~1000 cm−1 represents stretching vibration of CO bond in epoxy groups (C-O-C) [33]. It is not surprising to observe such a strong vibration for the hydroxyl groups in C-dots and GQD samples. Along with the hydroxyl groups present on the basal plane of the GQD molecules, surface hydroxyl groups react with GQDs to form more C-OH bonds [34]. This can be further confirmed from a broad absorption band present ~3400 cm−1 which corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH groups of water molecules adsorbed on GQDs and C-dots samples.

A strong absorption band ~1620 cm−1 can be attributed to the CC stretching vibrations which indicates the integrity of stable graphene structure. A small shoulder peak at ~1720 cm−1 is associated with CO stretching vibration of carboxylic group. The presence of carboxyl group can be further assured by a strong band present at ~1420 cm−1 which is associated with stretching vibrations of CO bond in carboxylic group [35]. A sharp peak present at ~620 cm−1 is characteristic of CH out-of-plane bending vibrations of benzene nuclei in the benzene ring [36]. Interestingly, the intensity of all these absorption bands observed to increase appreciably with increase in the temperature of synthesis. This indicates that amount of hydroxyl/alkoxy, carbonyl and carboxyl groups are increasing with increasing the temperature of synthesis. Presence of these groups also explains the hydrophilic nature of the synthesized mixture of C-dots and GQDs samples [37].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to determine the exact composition of the oxygen containing functional groups present in all the GQDs samples. Fig. 3 a, b and c shows the C-1s while d, e and f represent O-1s core level XPS spectra for GQD-85, GQD-100 and GQD-115 samples respectively. The high resolution XPS spectra of C-1s envelope of all GQDs and C-dots samples can be split into four gaussian peaks. Generally, the peak centered at 284.5 eV is assigned to graphitic sp2 carbon in (C=C) bond, peaks located at 285.8 and 287.6 eV represent sp3 carbon in (CO) bond in hydroxyl or alkoxy groups and carbonyl Carbon (C=O) bonds respectively, and the peak at 288.5 eV is attributed to the carboxylate group (HO-C=O) [38,39]. Percent proportions of these groups are listed in Table 1 .

Fig. 3d, e and f reveals that the O1s can be convoluted mainly into two peaks at ~531.2 eV and ~532.6 eV corresponding to CO and CO groups respectively [40]. The relative compositions of these groups are summarized in the Table 2 .

It can be noted from Table 1 that the major group (50–60%) present in all GQDs sample is Sp
2 carbon (C=C/C-C) which indicates the stability of graphitic structure in all GQDs and C-dots. As can be observed from the values in Tables 1 and 2, the content of hydroxyl/epoxy group tends to go down as temperature becomes higher. On the other hand, the content of carbonyl and carboxylic groups rises at higher temperatures. This indicates that no new carbonyl or carboxylic groups were formed but the hydroxyl/epoxy groups which are attached to the GQDs and C-dots further oxidized to carbonyl and carboxyl groups at higher temperatures. These results are further confirmed from the NEXAFS analysis of the GQDs samples.

NEXAFS is a powerful and ideal tool for the element-specific characterization of local atomic and electronic structure [41]. NEXAFS has the power to distinguish chemical and local bonding by providing structural information that does not appear in Raman, FTIR and XPS studies [42]. Fig. 4a and b shows C K-edge and O K-edge absorption spectra of mixture of C-dots and GQDs samples. All spectra were processed through pre-edge and post-edge normalization methods.

Fig. 4a indicates that NEXAFS spectra of the C K-edge consist of seven main characteristics features at ~285.6, ~286.8 eV, ~287.6, ~288.2, ~288.9, ~291 and ~293.7 eV. A characteristic sharp peak observed ~285.6 eV corresponds to the C 1s-π* transition which indicates the unbroken sp2 network in graphene. The lower intensity of GQD-115 can be attributed to the higher oxidation of these GQDS as oxidation may have caused the disruption of the inherent π-network upon sidewall functionalization [25]. A small feature ~286.8 eV (which is more visible in the inset in Fig. 4a) is specifically corresponds to the carbon single bonded to the oxygen in the hydroxyl group (π* C-OH states) [43]. The relatively smaller intensity of this feature confirms the low abundance of hydroxyl groups in the all the samples. The results indicated by NEXAFS is more reliable in this case as the measurements were taken in ultralow vacuum conditions which removes all surface bound water. Thus, the higher concentration of hydroxyl functionalities detected in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2d) must have major contributions from the surface hydroxyl groups forming (CO) bond with the graphitic carbon in C-dots and GQDs.

The third feature at ~287.6 eV represents convolution transition to CO states in epoxide moieties [44]. This feature is more prominent in GQD-85 and GQD-100 samples but diminished in GQD-115 sample. On the other hand, a very strong feature is observed only in GQD-115 sample at 288.2 eV which corresponds to the π* transitions of CO states. From these results we can conclude that out of the hydroxyl/epoxy groups (as indicated by XPS results), epoxy groups are preferably oxidizing to the carbonyl group at higher temperature.

A very strong peak at ~288.9 eV ascribed to the π* transitions for CO in carboxylic group is present in all the GQDs samples with more enhanced peak in GQD-115 sample which is well in agreement with XPS results. A well-structured peak at ~291 eV corresponds to the σ* bound exciton which arises from transitions to dispersionless unoccupied states possessing σ symmetry at the Γ point of the graphene Brillouin zone [45]. A broad structure at ~293 eV corresponds to the 1s-σ* transitions [46].

Valuable information about the degree of oxidation can be obtained from the O 1s spectra as shown in Fig. 4B. O K edge spectra of GQDs and C-dots showed five main characteristic peaks at ~530. 84, 532.2, 536.6, 538.1 and 542.1 eV. The lower absorption edges ~530. 84 and 532.2 are π* resonance from oxygen doubly bonded to carbon. In accordance with C 1s NEXAFS analysis, this peak appears to be broader and more intense for GQD-115 sample indicating higher contribution from (C=O) groups. The shoulder peaks appeared ~536.6 and 538.1 eV are attributed to σ* transitions for single bonded oxygen (CO) and (OH) respectively. The intensity of these peaks is significantly higher for GQD-85 sample as compared to the GQD-100 and GQD-115 samples. A broad peak prominently present at 542.1 eV in GQD-100 and GQD-115 samples is attributed to σ* transitions for CO groups [47].

Overall, NEXAFS spectra of C K-edge strongly suggest that the concentration of epoxy groups is higher than the hydroxyl groups in GQD-85 and GQD-100 samples. The spectral feature representing hydroxyl groups remains almost un-affected in all the GQDs samples whereas, peak correspond to epoxy group get diminished at higher temperatures (GQD-115) indicating preferable oxidation of epoxy groups to the carbonyl or carboxylic groups. The NEXAFS spectra of C K-edge and O K-edge also provided strong evidence for high concentration of carboxylic groups in GQD-115 sample which is not very clear from the FTIR and XPS analysis.

Based on the results obtained from TEM, AFM, FTIR, XPS and NEXAFS analysis, the mechanism of surface functionality formation on GQDs and C-dots can be explained. The schematic in Fig. 5 shows the varying degrees of oxidation of GQDs at different synthesis temperature.

The proposed first step in this reaction is the formation of highly oxidized graphene oxide (GO) from the graphene sheets. During the oxidation process, most of the carbonyl and carboxylic groups are formed at the edge plane (as reported from previous studies) Whereas, a large number of hydroxyl and epoxy groups are located closed to each other at the basal plane of graphene oxide thus creating more strain in the basal plane of GO sheets [48,49]. (As they are more in number) These epoxy groups formed have preference of aligning in a line on a carbon lattice [50]. This cooperative alignment induces a rupture of the underlying CC bond. Once an epoxy chain appears, it is energetically preferable for it to be further oxidized into epoxy pairs that then convert to more stable carbonyl and O−CO groups [51]. The rate of epoxy group rearrangement increases with an increase in the temperature forming smaller GO fragments and thus smaller GQDs. Smaller sized GQDs and C-dots have a larger number of carboxyl/carbonyl groups (attached to its edge plane) as compared to epoxy/hydroxyl groups (attached to its basal plane) [as indicated by GQD-100 and GQD-115 depictions in Fig. 5]. Therefore, at higher temperatures the percentage of carbonyl or carboxylic groups appears to be more than the hydroxyl/epoxy groups as evidently proven by NEXFAS and XPS results.

To investigate the effect of size and functional groups on the optical properties of C-dots and GQDs, UV–Vis and PL spectra were recorded and presented in Fig. 6 a and b respectively. UV–Vis spectra of mixtures of C-dots and GQDs samples show a typical absorption peak at ~280 nm which attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds [52]. Both C-dots and GQDs showed stronger absorption in the UV zone (280 nm to 350 nm), while its tail extends to the visible scope. In addition to that, GQDs a peak ranging from 360 to 410 nm which shows probable contribution to the n–p* transition of the CO bonds. This peak is considerably red shifted in GQD-85 sample. This red shift can be attributed to the larger size of dots which exhibits larger π-π conjugated domain than GQD-100 and GQD-115 [53].

The optical properties of the mixture of GQDs and C-dots were further investigated by PL spectra displayed in Fig. 6b. As can be observed from Fig. 6b, mixtures of C-dots and GQDs samples, exhibit strong emission at ~640 nm for an excitation wavelength of 580 nm. At this excitation wavelength, all the GQDs samples showed only red PL (inset of Fig. 6b). This indicates the size-independent PL behavior of the C-dots and GQDs. This can be attributed to the conjugated aromatic domains (isolated sp2 clusters) exist on the GQDs. According to the literature, small conjugated domains with various sizes (about 1–5 nm) coexisted as single particles which are physically connected to the GQDs. Although the sizes of the GQDs are different, the sizes of the sp
2 clusters might remain similar. Therefore, the light of a certain wavelength only excited sp
2 clusters with specific sizes, regardless of other components, leading to the size-independent PL [54,55].

It can be also noted from Fig. 6b that these C-based nanomaterials show significant variation in their emission intensities. This can be attributed to the different % of carbon dots present in that particular sample. It has been already reported that the PL quantum yields obtained from the bare C-dots is less than 10% because of its emissive traps on surface whereas, GQDs show greater quantum yield than the bare C-dots due to the presence of layers in their structure and better crystallinity. Hence, GQD-85 sample shows weaker PL signal which could be due to the higher % of carbon dots in it [56].

The size and surface functionality of the C-dots and GQDs have been successfully controlled by varying the synthesis temperature from 85°, 100° to 115 °C. XPS and NEXAFS study demonstrate that the degree of oxidation increases due to the oxidation of epoxy groups (at basal plane of GQDs) to carbonyl and carboxylic groups at higher temperatures. This conversion proceeds through the rupture of graphene oxide sheet forming smaller sized C-dots and GQDs. The percentage of C-dots is higher at lower temperature (GQD-85) which is confirmed by the AFM analysis. The resulting C-dots and GQDs shows size independent red shift in UV–Vis spectra. GQD-85 shows weaker PL emission than GQD-100 and GQD 115 sample which is associated with the higher percentage of C-dots in it.
Conception and design of study: Dr. Folarin Erogbogbo, Dr. Abraham Wolcott, Dr. Ozgur Keles
Acquisition of data: Anuja Bokare, Dr. Dennis Nordlund, Cynthia Melendrez, Ryan Robinson
Analysis and/or interpretation of data, Writing and reviewing: Anuja Bokare, Dr. Folarin Erogbogbo

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by San Jose State University Central Research, Scholarly and Creative Activity (RSCA) Program and the Charles W. Davidson College of Engineering.

Mater. Today
